chapters.summary: Council staff conduct pre-hearing preparations, confirm attendees, and remind those present to silence electronic devices and not approach the dais.
chapters.title: Staff housekeeping and hearing setup

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Is Gail coming?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Welcome to the New York City Council.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Vote for the coming out of sanitation and solid waste management.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please sign all self-electronic devices.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Moving forward, no one is to approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair Sanchez, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Justin Sanchez welcomes everyone and notes it is Budget Day, then restarts his opening remarks after a brief pause for water.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez opens hearing on Budget Day

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Happy Budget Day.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Oh.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: That's why I needed more water.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Alrighty, let's start that again.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone, and happy Budget Day.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Yay.

chapters.summary: Sanchez explains the committee is voting on Intro 31A, sponsored by Majority Leader Abreu, and Intro 92A by Councilmember Gale Brewer, both previously heard on May 19, 2026.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez states hearing purpose: vote on Intro 31A and Intro 92A

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Welcome to today's hearing by the Committee on Sanitation and Solid Waste Management.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: The purpose of this hearing is to hold a vote on Introduction 31A, sponsored by Majority Leader Shaun-Abreu, and Introduction 92A by Councilmember Gail Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: The committee previously heard these bills at a hearing on May 19, 2026, where it received testimonies from representatives of the Department of Sanitation and interested members of the public.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: If you weren't there, you missed out on some riveting action.

chapters.summary: Sanchez outlines how Intro 31A would require DSNY to designate additional covered establishments for organic waste separation by December 2026, with compliance tied to commercial waste zone rollout, and would require carters to provide food donation information.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez describes Intro 31A organic waste separation requirements

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Intro number 31A would require DSNY to, by December 1st, 2026, designate additional covered establishments to be subject to requirements regarding separation and disposal of organic waste.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Any establishment so designated would be required to comply with such requirements on the latter of June 1st, 2027, or when the establishment's commercial waste zone goes into effect.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: The bill would also require commercial waste zone awardee carters to provide information on the donation of excess edible food to any establishment.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: receiving organic waste collection.

chapters.summary: Sanchez reads Majority Leader Abreu's statement explaining that Intro 31A would require DSNY to review and designate additional business categories for organic waste separation, building on the city's growing organics system and household requirements.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez reads Abreu statement on expanding organic waste designation

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I will now read a statement on behalf of Council Member, Majority Leader, Abreu.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: You're welcome.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: For allowing me to speak on my bill.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: You have a lovely vase.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Intro 31A would require the Department of Sanitation to review and designate additional categories of businesses that may be subject to organic waste separation and disposal requirements.

chapters.summary: Continuing Abreu's statement, Sanchez describes how requiring more businesses to separate organics reduces landfill strain, lowers disposal costs under commercial waste zone rules, and fosters a more sustainable commercial waste system.
chapters.title: Abreu statement: environmental and cost benefits of organic waste separation

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: This will help ensure more establishments participate in our organic collections recycling system and practice responsible waste management.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: This bill...

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Builds on the growing success of our city's organics waste system, helping reduce strain on landfill and support our environmental goals.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Households are now required to separate their organic waste, and businesses should be held to the same standard.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: This bill is not just good for the environment, it's great.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: But also good for businesses'bottom lines by reducing waste disposal costs.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Under the city's current commercial waste zone rules, carters that collect commercial waste must charge less for organic waste collection than general refuse collection.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Requiring new types of establishments to separate organics will help save their businesses money as more of their waste is collected at the mandated lower rates.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Ultimately, it will cost businesses less to do the right thing for our environment.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Intro 31A will help foster a more sustainable and environmental-friendly commercial waste system in our city, and I look forward to working with my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: to move this bill forward.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I don't know why there's a pause there, but there is, so I'll say it out loud, pause.

chapters.summary: Sanchez introduces Intro 92A, which would permit DSNY, DOT, and designated agencies to place stickers on vehicle windows to enforce alternate side parking rules, joking that the stickers will be sticky and hated.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez introduces Intro 92A on alternate side parking stickers

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I want to say thank you to Councilmember Gail Brewer for joining us.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And that brings us to Intro 92A, which would permit the Department of Transportation, DSNY, and any department designated by DSNY to place a sticker.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: No larger than 8.5 by 11 inches on the window of any motor vehicle as a method of enforcing alternate side parking rules.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: It will be sticky.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: You will hate it.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: It will suck.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Move your cars.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I will now turn it over to Councilmember Brewer to provide a statement on her bill.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Gale Brewer recounts how DSNY began placing day-glow green stickers in 1987 on vehicles with repeated alternate side violations, calling them one of the city's most effective tools for keeping streets clean before the Council banned them in 2012.
chapters.title: Brewer on history of sanitation stickers as effective street cleaning tool

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Bernacoff is very nice to switch for a minute.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This morning I'm very nice.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So even though she hates the stickers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So thank you very much, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Chair.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I have to say in 87, sanitation started placing day glow green stickers on vehicles, those who had repeated alternate side violations.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That was because things were not being cleaned on the street.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: These stickers are credited as one of the city's single most effective tools for helping keep our streets clean for all New Yorkers.

chapters.summary: Brewer explains that after the 2012 ban, many drivers calculate it is cheaper to risk a $65 ticket than pay for garage parking, citing a 2026 DSNY survey estimating half a million cars violate street cleaning regulations weekly, preventing cleaning of over 3,000 miles of streets.
chapters.title: Brewer on 2012 sticker ban and scope of alternate side violations

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Then after the council, and I must admit, I think I probably voted for it, banned the stickers in 2012, too many people make the following calculation.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's cheaper to not move your car for alternate side parking and risk paying the $65 parking ticket than to pay for a garage.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In April 26 survey, the Department of Sanitation estimated half a million cars violated street cleaning regulations on over 1,200 routes each week.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Preventing the cleaning of more than 3,000 miles of streets.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Street cleaning works when cars move.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It doesn't work otherwise.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The sanitation sweepers cannot clean the curbling.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They weave in and out of parked cars while litter, debris, and grime build up.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I believe it's time to restore this tool to improve compliance and help keep streets clean for all New Yorkers.

chapters.summary: Brewer argues for restoring stickers to improve compliance, notes she would prefer cameras on sanitation sweepers if Albany passed enabling legislation, thanks Speaker Menon, Chair Sanchez, DSNY leadership, and her staff, and closes by urging New Yorkers to move their cars.
chapters.title: Brewer on restoring stickers, camera alternative, and acknowledgments

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I have to say,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: to say would have been great if the state passed the cameras on the sanitation sweepers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Norman Stysel, a former member of the commissioner of sanitation, wrote in the Daily News yesterday that's what he would prefer, but it did not happen.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We need the stickers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want to thank Speaker Menon.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want to thank Sanitation Chair Sanchez for their support.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: As well as Morgan Barrett, Joanna Castro, Andrew Vasquez, Jeff Baker, and of course, in my office, Cynthia Hornig and Shula Puder.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I also want to thank the Commissioner Anderson from Sanitation, former Commissioner Logan.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And New York's strongest for their vocal support of this legislation.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They've already started making stickers and their tireless efforts to keep our city clean.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know some people don't like this.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What I say is tell me another way to move those damn cars.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I also say that if the Albany finally passes the cameras whenever, then this would only be for the most egregious cars.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You don't pay extra for a sticker.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You just can't get the damn thing off.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so you will be targeted as you did not move your car.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I love on my block to put stickers up because nobody moves.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Sanchez thanks Brewer and turns the proceedings over to Committee Clerk William Martin to conduct a roll call vote on Intro 31A and Intro 92A, which are coupled together.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez hands to clerk for roll call vote on both bills

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And with that, I will now turn it over to the clerk to take the vote on Enchos 31A and 92A.

chapters.summary: Clerk William Martin conducts the roll call. Chair Sanchez, Councilmembers Louis, Hanif, Ung, Zhuang, Hankerson, Maloney, Santosuosso, Thomas-Henry, and Wilson vote aye; Councilmember Vernikov votes no.
chapters.title: Roll call vote on Intro 31A and Intro 92A

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: William Martin, Committee Clerk, Roll Call Vote Committee on Sanitation and Solid Waste Management.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Both items are coupled.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Chair Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Aye.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Lewis.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Aye.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Hanif.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Ong.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Ung
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Zhuang.

utterances.speaker: Susan Zhuang
utterances.text: Aye.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Hankerson.

utterances.speaker: Ty Hankerson
utterances.text: Aye.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Maloney.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Aye.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Santos-Suosto.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Aye.

utterances.speaker: Kayla Santosuosso
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Thomas Henry.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Aye.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Wilson.

utterances.speaker: Carl Wilson
utterances.text: Aye.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Vernikov.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: No and all.

chapters.summary: Clerk Martin announces the vote of 10 in the affirmative and 1 in the negative with no abstentions, adopting both items. Chair Sanchez closes the hearing and reminds members of the next hearing tomorrow.
chapters.title: Vote result: 10-1, both Intro 31A and 92A adopted by committee

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: With a vote of 10 in the affirmative, 1 in the negative, and no abstentions, both items have been adopted by the committee.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Mr.

utterances.speaker: William Martin
utterances.text: Chair, that is a full committee.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And I will see my members of the Sanitation Committee tomorrow for our next hearing.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, everyone.

summary: Committee on Sanitation and Solid Waste Management holds a hearing to vote on Intro 31A (expanding organic waste separation requirements for businesses) and Intro 92A (restoring parking stickers for alternate side parking enforcement).

Chair Sanchez reads a statement from Majority Leader Abreu on the environmental and cost benefits of Intro 31A, and Councilmember Gale Brewer advocates for Intro 92A citing the history of sticker effectiveness and widespread non-compliance with alternate side parking.
Both bills pass committee with a 10-1 vote, with Councilmember Vernikov voting no.
title: Committee on Sanitation and Solid Waste Management
